signals.reasoning: Existing ETH long remains a small bounded-risk management hold, and with the veto now cleared there is even less reason to force an exit while the position is still manageable. The live ETH exposure stays the cleanest thing to manage rather than rotate away from.

BTC is still consolidating after the prior squeeze and does not offer a clearly better management alternative than the current ETH position. With the board clear it improves from watch-only, but not enough to justify a switch.
SOL is broadly stable but still moving with the same major-crypto tape. It is not cleaner than the existing ETH hold for position-management purposes.

TST remains a noisy momentum name after its squeeze-style move, making it less attractive for disciplined management than the current small ETH long. Clear board is not enough to make it a better rotation.
STBL can still look mechanically interesting, but it remains unstable enough that it does not outrank the existing ETH exposure. Keep it on the watchlist rather than rotate into it.
FOGO is still a thin high-beta name where execution quality can deteriorate quickly. Even with veto cleared, it is not a cleaner management choice than ETH.
PEOPLE looks more like recovery noise than a clean new trend. It stays in the hold bucket because the existing ETH position is still the more straightforward exposure to manage.
XPL may be interesting on relative movement, but it is still less established than the live ETH position and not strong enough to justify rotation. Hold only.
MAVIA can move sharply, but that volatility cuts both ways and makes it a lower-quality management switch from ETH. Better kept as watch-only.
LDO still has a constructive rebound structure, but not enough separation to justify leaving the current ETH hold. Clear board helps, but not enough to change the ranking.
MERL remains in rebound mode without becoming a clearly superior opportunity. It does not justify rotating away from the open ETH position.
